content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

October 17, 1991


Mr. Louis N. Haddad
5 Deer Stalker Path
Monterey, CA 93940

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-91-394

Dear Mr. Haddad:

     This is in response to your request for guidance, in
connection with the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 According
to a telephone conversation we had in August, 1991, you conceded
that the question in your letter referred to past conduct; you
were informed by me at that time that the Legal Division attorneys
are prohibited from commenting on past conduct, but that we could
cite applicable law for you for purposes of guidance in the future.
Hence, this letter is in the nature of informal assistance.2

QUESTIONS

     1.   May more than two hundred pieces of campaign literature
be circulated door-to-door, with no indication on the literature
itself of the identification of the circulators?

     2.   When a city reimburses an elected city official for
salary lost from private employment because the official is
attending to city business, is that reimbursement reportable?




___________________________
1    Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2    Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

CONCLUSIONS

1. Campaign literature circulated door-to-door is not
subject to the mass mailing requirements for sender identification
as set forth in Section 84305.

2. Reimbursement from a local, state, or federal
governmental entity is exempted from the definition of income
found in Section 82030; such reimbursement would not, therefore,
be reportable.

FACTS

You were an unsuccessful candidate in a municipal election,
and you are contemplating another attempt to gain public office.
In a telephone conversation subsequent to receipt of
your letter requesting guidance, you conceded that your questions
related to past conduct, but stated that you would appreciate
general guidance as to applicable law in connection with your
questions. You have been advised that questions of past conduct
should be addressed to the Enforcement Division of the Commission,
and the advice rendered below is for prospective purposes only.

ANALYSIS

Mass Mailing is defined in Section 82041.5 as follows:

"Mass mailing" means over two hundred
substantially similar pieces of mail, but does not
include a form letter or other mail which is sent
in response to an unsolicited request, letter or
other inquiry.

The requirements for a mass mailing of campaign literature
are found in Section 84305.

(a) Except as provided in subdivision (b), no
candidate or committee shall send a mass mailing
unless the name, street address, and city of the
candidate or committee are shown on the outside of
each piece of mail in the mass mailing and on at
least one of the inserts included within each piece
of mail of the mass mailing in no less than 6-point type
which shall be in a color or print which contrasts
with the background so as to be easily legible. A
post office box may be stated in lieu of a street
address if the organization's address is a matter
of public record with the Secretary of State.

(b) If the sender of the mass mailing is a
single candidate or committee, the name, street
address, and city of the candidate or committee

need only be shown on the outside of each piece of
mail.

(c) If the sender of a mass mailing is a
controlled committee, the name of the person
controlling the committee shall be included in
addition to the information required by subdivision
(a).

We have previously advised that door-to-door delivery of
campaign literature would not be subject to the sender
identification requirements listed in Section 84305. (Ennis
Advice Letter No. 78-110.) Therefore, the Act would not require
the identification of the circulators of campaign literature
delivered door-to-door.

As to your question regarding reimbursement from a city to an
elected city official for salary lost because of time spent by the
official on city business, we refer you to the definition of
income found in Section 82030 of the Act. Subdivision (b) of that
provision states:

(b) "Income" also does not include:

* * *

(2) Salary and reimbursement for expenses or per
diem received from a state, local, or federal
government agency ..."

Since Section 82030 excludes salary and reimbursement from a
local government agency, the reimbursement paid by your city to
the elected official would not be reportable as income under the
Act.

There may be other areas of the law which may affect this
issue. Our advice is limited to interpretation of the Act and
does not address whether such reimbursement should be provided by
the city.

As noted above, any concerns over specific past conduct
should be addressed to the Enforcement Division of the Commission.
If we can assist you in the future in connection with your rights
and duties under the Act, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Sincerely,

SCOTT HALLABRIN
Acting General Counsel

Susan L. Borrow
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:SLB

2. When a city reimburses an elected city official for salary lost from private employment because the official is attending to city business, is that reimbursement reportable?

2. When a city reimburses an elected city official for salary lost from private employment because the official is attending to city business, is that reimbursement reportable?

, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
